name: Denmark
summary: Structurally world-class — WJP rule of law #1, GPI 8th, Henley 3rd passport, a 3-year open post-study work permit, immediate free healthcare via CPR, strong tech salaries and dual citizenship retained both ways (allowed since 2015; Denmark on Bangladesh's DNC list) — yet a poor fit for a Muslim Bangladeshi couple targeting citizenship: Denmark runs Europe's most restrictive migration regime (Social-Democrat 'zero asylum', 'parallel society'/ghetto laws keyed to 'non-Western' origin, an expanding burqa/niqab ban, recurrent Quran-burnings), naturalisation takes 9 years with B2 Danish and self-sufficiency, permanent residence effectively 8 years, and tax is among the world's highest. Derived overall sits just below the 60% inclusion line.

links.title: New to Denmark — official immigration portal (SIRI/nyidanmark)
Life in Denmark — Danish citizenship conditions (borger.dk, official)

job-market.summary: High absolute tech salaries in Copenhagen with AI/ML the fastest-rising specialty and skilled-migration channels open (Positive List, Pay Limit scheme), though the market is Copenhagen-centric.

pros.text: High tech pay: software engineers in Copenhagen earn ~DKK 600k–880k total comp and AI/ML engineers ~DKK 600k–1,000k+, with AI/ML the fastest-rising specialty.
Skilled migration channels (Positive List, Pay Limit scheme) keep employer routes open for qualified graduates.

cons.text: The market is Copenhagen-centric (Aarhus/Odense secondary), limiting depth for two simultaneous tech earners.
Danish is often expected outside international/MNC roles, and integration politics colour hiring sentiment for non-Western newcomers.

links.title: Levels.fyi — Software Engineer salary, Copenhagen
Jobicy — AI Engineer salary, Denmark (2025)

visa-access.summary: Bangladeshi students lodge at the VFS Denmark centre in Dhaka with SIRI deciding, but funds proof is significant (~DKK 100,000/yr plus tuition) and processing runs 2–3 months.

pros.text: Applications are lodged at the VFS Global Denmark centre in Dhaka (no travel to a third country), with SIRI deciding the residence permit.
No Bangladesh-specific student-visa ban; skilled/student channels remain open despite the harsh asylum regime.

cons.text: Proof of funds is significant — ~DKK 100,000/yr (~€13,400) plus full tuition — documented over 3 months of statements.
Processing typically takes 2–3 months with no student fast-track.

links.title: New to Denmark — apply as a student (official)
Study in Denmark — residence permits (non-EU/EEA, official portal)

citizenship.summary: Dual citizenship is retained both ways and the passport is elite (Henley 3rd), but naturalisation is one of Europe's hardest: 9 years' residence, B2 Danish, a citizenship test and 2 years of self-sufficiency, granted by parliamentary act.

pros.text: Dual citizenship works both ways: Denmark has allowed it since September 2015 and Denmark is on Bangladesh's DNC list, so a naturalising Bangladeshi keeps BD nationality.
The Danish passport ranks 3rd on Henley 2026 (~186 destinations).

cons.text: Naturalisation requires ~9 years' residence, a B2 Danish exam (Prøve i Dansk 3), the citizenship test (indfødsretsprøven) and 2 years of self-sufficiency with no public benefits — among the strictest in Europe.
Citizenship is ultimately granted by parliamentary act, adding discretion on top of the statutory bars.

links.title: Life in Denmark — conditions for acquiring Danish citizenship (official)

post-study-work.summary: A standout strength: graduates of a Danish bachelor/master/PhD get a 3-year work permit without limitations and no job offer required, and family can accompany — matching Canada's best-in-class runway.

pros.text: Since the 2023 reform, graduates of a Danish bachelor/master/PhD receive a 3-year residence-and-work permit without limitations and with no job offer required — among the most generous PSW windows anywhere.
The permit allows the family to accompany, and each graduating spouse can hold their own.

cons.text: Conversion to long-term skilled work still needs a qualifying job (Pay Limit/Positive List), and the route depends on Danish-market placement.

links.title: New to Denmark — establishment / graduate work permit (official)
Study-Abroad — Working in Denmark 2026 (stay-back)

spouse-family.summary: The graduate's 3-year permit lets family accompany with spouse work rights and free schooling, but integration/ghetto laws impose conditions, and Denmark is restrictive on family reunification generally.

pros.text: A graduate's 3-year work permit allows the spouse and children to accompany, with spouse work access; if both spouses study, each holds their own permit and PSW.
Public schooling is free and high quality.

cons.text: Denmark applies strict family-reunification rules generally, and 'parallel society'/ghetto laws impose integration conditions (e.g. mandatory daycare hours) on immigrant-background families.

links.title: New to Denmark — accompanying family members (official)

msc-study.summary: Most master's are taught in English at strong universities (DTU, Copenhagen, Aarhus) and qualify for the 3-year PSW, but non-EU tuition is high (DTU ~€30k over two years) with limited waivers.

pros.text: Most Danish master's are taught in English at strong universities (DTU, University of Copenhagen, Aarhus) with deep CS/AI offerings, and a master's qualifies for the 3-year PSW.
Students may work ~20 hrs/week during term (full-time in summer); a Bangladeshi BSc-CSE is admissible at the graduate level.

cons.text: Non-EU tuition is high (~€7,200–8,000/yr at Copenhagen/Aarhus; ~€30,000 over two years at DTU) with only a very limited number of tuition-fee waivers.

links.title: DTU — Fees and funding (official)
Study.eu — Master's in Denmark (2026)

pr-pathway.summary: Permanent residence is demanding by EU standards — typically 8 years (reducible to 4 only with strong employment and integration), Danish-language and self-support conditions.

pros.text: The 3-year graduate permit plus skilled employment build toward permanent residence, and the route is well documented.

cons.text: Permanent residence normally requires 8 years' residence (reducible to 4 only with strong employment and integration conditions) plus Danish-language and self-support tests — stricter than the typical EU 5-year route.
The restrictive policy direction reduces predictability for non-Western applicants.

links.title: New to Denmark — permanent residence permit (official)

income-cost.summary: High salaries are offset by some of the world's highest taxes and expensive Copenhagen living, leaving a moderate surplus; the study phase is costly.

pros.text: High gross tech salaries support a reasonable surplus and easy remittance once employed in skilled roles.

cons.text: Income tax reaches ~57% (60.5% with labour-market tax), and Copenhagen living and housing are expensive, compressing net surplus.
The study phase is costly — high tuition plus a ~DKK 100,000 funds requirement and limited part-time hours.

links.title: PwC — Denmark: taxes on personal income

healthcare.summary: A clear strength: registering for a CPR number (stays over three months) gives immediate free access to the universal Danish health service, with strong welfare and childcare.

pros.text: Students and workers staying over three months get a CPR number giving immediate, free access to the universal national health service — no waiting period or private insurance needed once registered.
Strong Nordic welfare and heavily subsidised childcare.

cons.text: GP and elective-treatment waits exist, and full coverage begins only after CPR registration on arrival.

links.title: Study in Denmark — Healthcare (official portal)

culture-language.summary: Danes' English is excellent and Copenhagen is highly livable, but Danish is required for status (B2 for citizenship), and integration politics make the climate cool for non-Western Muslim newcomers.

pros.text: Danes have excellent English, so daily life and work are largely workable in English in the cities, and livability/work-life balance are world-leading.

cons.text: Danish is required for permanent residence and at B2 for citizenship — a steep, long-term burden.
Assimilationist integration politics and the 'non-Western' framing make social acceptance cool for visibly Muslim South-Asian newcomers.

links.title: How to Live in Denmark — Muslims in Denmark

safety-law.summary: Top of the world for rule of law (WJP 2025 #1) and among the safest (GPI 8th), but discriminatory-by-design laws (ghetto/parallel-society rules, burqa ban) and recurrent Quran-burnings undercut protection for Muslims.

pros.text: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: 1st of 143 worldwide; Global Peace Index 2025: 8th — extremely safe, low corruption, reliable courts and strong women's safety.

cons.text: Recurrent public Quran-burnings (170+ anti-Muslim protests in a single month) and a discriminatory legal posture — the burqa/niqab ban and 'non-Western'-keyed ghetto laws — undercut protection for Muslim residents.
Immigration enforcement is harsh (revocable permits, 95% removal of rejected claims).

links.title: World Justice Project — Denmark ranks 1st (2025 profile)
Vision of Humanity — Global Peace Index 2025

politics.summary: Very stable institutions, but a Social-Democrat government runs Europe's most restrictive migration regime — 'zero asylum', revocable protection, and 'parallel society'/ghetto laws explicitly keyed to 'non-Western' origin.

pros.text: Institutions are very stable, and skilled-worker/student channels (Positive List, Pay Limit scheme, graduate permit) remain open and functional.

cons.text: The mainstream Social-Democrat government has normalised the hardest migration line in Europe: a stated 'zero asylum seekers' goal, revocable temporary protection, and family-reunification restrictions.
'Parallel society'/ghetto laws use 'non-Western' origin as a legal category and the burqa/niqab ban targets Muslim practice — direct state-level pressure on Muslim South-Asian residents.

links.title: Immigration policy of Mette Frederiksen (overview)
Migration Policy Institute — Denmark, a pioneer in restrictive policy

tax.summary: Among the world's highest taxes (~57%, 60.5% with labour-market tax); a 27% researcher scheme exists but its high salary threshold puts it largely out of reach for newly graduated engineers, and no Bangladesh treaty is confirmed.

pros.text: A researcher/highly-paid expat scheme allows a flat 27% (32.84% with labour-market tax) for up to 7 years — substantial relief for those who qualify.

cons.text: Ordinary tax reaches ~57% (60.5% with labour-market tax), and the researcher scheme's high salary threshold (~DKK 65,400/month, ~€105k/yr) is largely out of reach for newly graduated engineers.
No Denmark–Bangladesh double-taxation treaty is confirmed, leaving repatriation relief uncertain.

links.title: Skat (Danish Tax Agency) — Tax scheme for researchers (official)

muslim-diaspora.summary: A ~5% Muslim population with 150+ mosques and good halal infrastructure in Copenhagen/Aarhus, but a hostile sentiment and legal climate — recurrent Quran-burnings, a burqa/niqab ban and ghetto laws.

pros.text: Muslims are ~5.1% (~300,000) with 150+ mosques and Islamic centres and good halal food/grocery/butcher availability in Copenhagen (Nørrebro), Aarhus and Odense.

cons.text: The sentiment climate is hostile — recurrent public Quran-burnings (Paludan; 170+ anti-Muslim protests in a month) drew formal Bangladeshi government condemnation.
Legal accommodation is poor: a public burqa/niqab ban (expanding to schools/universities) and 'ghetto' laws targeting non-Western communities; the specific Bangladeshi community is small.

links.title: Islam in Denmark (overview)
Dhaka Tribune — Bangladesh condemns Quran burning in Copenhagen

direct-work-route.summary: Open front door, slow climb. White-collar routes exist (Pay Limit, Positive List for Higher Education, Fast-Track, EU Blue Card) and IT roles are explicitly shortage-listed into 2026–27, with the Positive List carrying no fixed salary floor — a good fit for the couple's BSc + experience. But the salary bar is high and rising (2026 Pay Limit DKK 552,000; supplementary DKK 446,000), and crucially Bangladesh is NOT among the 16 favoured countries given the eased DKK 300,000 threshold (India/China/etc. are) — so a BD applicant pays the full bar peers escape. The onward path is among Europe's hardest: PR typically 8 years (4 only with all supplementary conditions) and citizenship ~9 years with Danish at B2 and tests. Dual citizenship is allowed (since 2015), so BD nationality is retained.

pros.text: IT/software occupations are explicitly on the Positive List for Higher Education (no fixed salary floor) into 2026–27; Pay Limit/Fast-Track/Blue Card are nationality-blind; dual citizenship retained (since 2015).
Copenhagen has a genuine tech/AI shortage and a streamlined Fast-Track for certified employers (≥10 staff).

cons.text: Bangladesh is excluded from the 16-country DKK 300,000 threshold easing — a BD applicant must clear the full DKK 446,000–552,000 salary bar that India/China/etc. avoid.
Onward path among Europe's hardest: PR typically 8 years, citizenship ~9 years with Danish B2 + tests; negligible BD diaspora/track record in Denmark.
